Is that an impressive slide or what?
The shot across the bow is an old naval term that’s still used. Say a ship of war is
chasing down another ship, it might shoot a shot across the bow, literally a canon shot
in front of the boat that is fleeing from them. It’s basically a warning to stop, “we’ve got
our guns trained on you, stop or we’re going to fire on you.” It’s basically a warning shot
just to warn the boat that is trying to run away.
SELLING OF YOUR SEED LAUNCH… IN 5 PARTS
1. THE SHOT ACROSS THE BOW

The shot across the bow within the context of your launch is a warning, it’s a way of
letting people know that something is coming. The way the shot across the bow works is
you’re asking for interaction with your list. I’m going to show you a couple of different
ways to ask for that interaction.
As part of that interaction there’s this, “Oh by the way, we’re also going to be rolling out
this thing,” so it’s basically a way of telling people that something is coming without
asking for the sale, without telling them you’re going to sell them, and it’s a way of
engaging them in the process.
I have two different models to show you that I’ve used for a shot across the bow. I’ve
got a classic that I’ve used many times and it works really well. Then I have the call for
case studies, which I’ve used a couple times. And I actually used it as part of this seed
launch, my launch of the Seed Launch Deep Dive I used the call for case studies.
There are lots of other possibilities and I’ve used other variations, but these two are very
simple and they’re going to work really well, they’re going to work 95% of the time.
Sometimes I’ll change up my personal shot across the bow because I’ve been teaching
this stuff so all my students sort of know my tricks, so just to shake things up I’ll change
things up. But the classic and the call for case studies are going to work for you.
Really, the classic is going to work 80% of the time and then maybe 15% of the time
use the call for case studies. Then if you really want to get creative once you’ve done
this a few times, feel free to get creative.
Shot Across The Bow Checklist
Just as a checklist with the shot across the bow.
First of all, it’s very friendly. People are usually very enthusiastic to get these messages,
to help you out, to take your surveys, to give you case studies. It establishes authority. It
arouses curiosity, which is really what the shot across the bow is all about is getting
people interested in what’s coming up.
You also look for feedback. You’ve seen throughout this whole seed launch how I’m so
tied into getting feedback. It helps you design a great product and it helps you design a
great launch.
Also, it doesn’t sell. This is basically a way of letting people know that something is
coming without selling. And people support that which they help create. You’re enlisting
the help of your list in creating the product, as you’ll see in just a minute.

When you do that people are inherently somewhat invested in the process and they’ll
tend to support it, they’ll tend to buy from you, they’ll tend to tell other people about it,
they might even share it in social media. That’s sort of the overall checklist.
The “Classic” Shot Across the Bow
The class shot across the bow basically comes down to asking your list, “What are you
top two questions about _______?”
It all starts with a very simple email. This is one that I did years and years ago before I
was teaching internet marketing, this is when I was teaching about the stock market and
this is the actual email that I sent. It’s a very simple short email.
The subject line was “Big News. Need Your Help, Please.” You could actually have the
subject line be “Need Your Help, Please,” and leave off the “big news.”
Then it says, “After literally years of work, I’m putting the finishing touches on my long
awaited trading manual. This thing has been a labor of love, but at times it has felt like
giving birth. However, now that the finish line is in sight I could really use your help.”
This is that idea of enlisting their help, that people will support that which they help
create. Then I go to say, “You see, I want to make sure that I’ve covered everything, but
I’m a little too close to this project right now. Now that I’m almost finished I’d like to get
some feedback from you. This will only take you a few seconds. Can you go to this link
and answer this one simple question?”
It’s a very simple, very direct email. Right in the beginning it tells people that I’m close to
finishing this, I’ve been working on it for so long, and it’s almost ready to go. Right there
it lets them know that something is coming, but this email isn’t about selling and there’s
not a hint of a sale in here. It’s more about seeking their feedback.
Survey
Then this is the survey. That link sent them to a survey and this what the survey looked
like.
The survey again reiterates that “we’re very close to finishing the long awaited training
manual, we’ve been working on this for four years, we’ll be releasing it in early January,”
so now we’re starting to give them a little bit more details. “The course will be entirely
focused on support and resistance,” which people that are in the trading market know
what that is and it’s sort of a hot button for them.

Prelaunch Checklist
First, you want to establish authority.
You want to provide value in your prelaunch.
You want to paint the future, show the possibility of transformation. There’s a big piece
here where you’re showing people that they can transform their lives, that they can get
the results in their lives.
You want to foreshadow the offer towards the end of your prelaunch.
Prelaunch Triggers
The triggers you want to hit in here are authority. Authority means if someone perceives
you an authority it will be much easier to influence them. Think of a doctor walking in a
white coat, that gives them instant authority. Uniforms can give authority. Past results
can give authority. One of the ways we’re going to give authority here is just by giving
good content. People see you giving good content and they think, “Holy cow, they really
know what they’re talking about.”
The second one is likability. We’re much likely to buy something from someone that we
know, like, and trust. This process is a very likable process, even the shot across the
bow. It’s just inherently a likable process. You’re asking people for their help, you’re
asking people for their feedback, you’re willing to pay attention to them because you’re
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 46 --

10
©Internet Alchemy Inc. 2013
All Rights Reserved.
asking them to talk to you. You’re in full on listening mode. People love it when you
listen to them. It’s not a hard sell, it’s just very inherently likable.
Then there’s social proof. Social proof is the idea if we see a lot of people doing
something then we’re going to be more inclined to do that as well.
This is like if you go to a download site, maybe you want to download some software to
help you edit videos. You go to Download.com and there are 100 different programs
that will help you edit videos. One of them has been downloaded 1.2 million times, one
of them has been downloaded 40,000 times, and the rest have all been downloaded
2,000 times or less. Which one are you going to download first?
It’s going to cost you the time and the effort to download it, install the program and
check it out, so you want to reduce your risk as much as possible. Which one are you
going to download? You’re probably going to start with the one that has over a million
downloads. All those other people must know something, right?
That’s social proof. Basically we look to others to help us guide our actions. We do that
to reduce the risk in our lives, to reduce the friction, to be more time efficient. It’s a very
powerful trigger, so we will hit that in our prelaunch.
Then scarcity. The idea is if something is more scarce, then we want it more. It’s just
one of the most powerful mental triggers there is, it’s built into all of us. If there is less of
something, we naturally want it more. That’s just the way of the world.
This next one I’m going to show you, I don’t want this to hold you back. This is almost
an aside.
Launch List or Not?
That’s the idea of whether you want to create what we call a launch list or not.
Sometimes when we are in a launch we will take our list and we’ll direct them to a
squeeze page to see if they want to opt-in to the launch list. The launch list will get all
the promotions and all the emails for that launch.
I know a lot of times these are the type of questions that bog people down and stop
them. Please, do not let this stop you. I’m going to talk about it for a minute, but I’m sure
you’ve heard the Pareto principle, the 80/20 rule. Everything I’m talking about with the
shot across the bow, the prelaunch content, and with the sales video, that’s the 20%
that will get you the 80% of results. Whether to do a launch list or not is a very minor
question that’s not hugely going to move the needle. This is completely optional whether
or not to have a launch list.

If you haven’t launched before and your current list is closely in alignment with the seed
launch offer, you do not use a launch list. You do not. In other words, if people have
been coming to your site and it’s all about playing guitar and now you’re going to put out
a product about learning how to play guitar, don’t use a launch list.
Especially if you haven’t launched before. It’s very simple. If people have joined your list
in the general area, in the general topic and you haven’t been hitting them with lots of
different offers, then just go ahead and use your entire list, don’t use a launch list.
Now, if the seed launch is a different type of offer than why they joined your list, then
you use a launch list. If people have been coming to your site and it’s all about sheet
music, where to get sheet music, and how to find the best sheet music, and everything
about sheet music, and now you want to teach people how to play guitar, that’s really a
different type offer and you would drive them to a launch list. I’m going to show you how
to do that in just a second. It’s very simple.

Quick checklist on the sale:
 Remember, you’re selling the transformation. I know I’ve said that before, I’m
going to keep on saying it.
 You want to hit authority.
 You want to hit scarcity.
 You want to do a “this is for you if,” telling people who it’s for and making sure
they understand that it’s for them.
 You want a strong presentation of price.
One of the things about pricing is that one of the limiting factors is the price that you can
ask for with a straight face, the price that you can ask for knowing that you deserve it,
the price that you can ask for knowing that it’s worth it, the price that you can ask for
knowing that it’s a fair trade for the transformation you’re going to make in someone’s
life.
When you present that price, you have to do it with authority, you have to do it like it’s
just another day in the park, like you’re walking up to Starbucks and ordering a latte.

You have to have scarcity. Do not make an offer without scarcity. You absolutely have

I’m showing you seed launches and in seed launches the scarcity is really simple
because it’s a live event, it’s going to start and it’s going to end. Since it has a defined

Question, “Can you put bonuses before price?”
You could. I like to put them after the price, because then it makes them separate, it
makes them really feel like a bonus, at least in the way that we’re laying it out here.
There are plenty of sales letters where the bonuses are put in before price, but the deal
with sales letters is that people scroll down to the bottom to look at the price before they
read the sales letter anyway so there’s no surprising them with the price.
Here I like to make the offer, give the price, and then come back and stack on the
bonuses. Now what you’ve done is you already justified the price, then you give them
price and they’re already thinking, “That might be pretty good,” and they’re trying to
judge the value. They’re trying to figure out if that price is worth what they’re getting.
Then you come back and you add in something extra and all of a sudden it takes that
scale and shifts it further. That’s why I like to put it after.

start. So they have to buy before then to get in. That’s your hard deadline, that’s your

In my case, I had additional scarcity because we only had a limited number of spots.
We ended up closing down on Thursday and we didn’t actually do the first call until the
next Tuesday. But, if I hadn’t filled all of those spots up, I could have let it run up until
Sunday or Monday because we were going to start on Tuesday. Then it would have
been, “Hey, you have to buy now, we’re about to start the calls.”
It’s just like a college class. You can’t register for a college class two weeks after the
class starts, you have to register before the class starts. That just makes sense. So you
absolutely need that hard deadline.
Now, half of your orders are going to come in on your closing day. That’s why you need
scarcity.
Again, if you don’t want to sell very much product, you don’t have to have scarcity. If
you want to be effective, you need scarcity. Scarcity comes from the hard deadline.
On your closing day you absolutely need to send two emails. You absolutely have to. I
see people not do this and they leave a lot of money on the table. In fact, you’ll lose
approximately 25% of your sales if you don’t send that second email on your closing
day.
Make the deadline at Midnight. Don’t make the deadline at 10:00 in the morning, at
Noon, or at 2:00 in the afternoon. If you’re in the U.S. and you’re selling to U.S. people,
make it Midnight Eastern or Midnight Pacific. For me, I’m in the U.S. but 40% of my
orders come from outside the U.S., from all over the world. There are lots of people on
the call right now from Australia, lots of people from Europe.
If you’re in the U.S., make it Midnight Eastern or Midnight Pacific, depending on when
you want to go to bed. If you’re in Eastern and you make the deadline Pacific, you have
to stay up until 3:00 in the morning to take it down so that’s a bit of a pain and you’ll
want to make it Eastern. Since I’m almost in Pacific, I usually do Pacific Time.
If you’re in Poland and you’re selling to people in Poland, make it Midnight your local
time. If you’re in Australia and you’re selling mostly to people in Australia, make it
Midnight your local time. If you’re in Australia and 60% of your market is in the U.S.,
make it Midnight Eastern or Midnight Pacific.

Question from Diane, “I was thinking of making my five modules the whole product I

Your product, if you do it exactly how I’m teaching you, you’re going to do five calls. Just

something that you can go deep enough to really provide value and do it in five calls.

They should be as long as they need to be and not a minute longer. I know that sounds
